modules.title: Electrostatics
modules.html:
Like charges repel; unlike attract. Charge is quantised: q = ne (e = 1.6×10⁻¹⁹ C).

Coulomb's law: F = kq₁q₂/r², k = 9×10⁹ N·m²/C².

Electric field E = F/q. Electric potential V = W/q (volt).

Capacitor: Q = CV. Energy = ½CV². Units: farad (F).

examples.q: Force between +2 μC and +3 μC, 0.5 m apart.
Charge on 10 μF capacitor across 12 V?

modules.title: Current and Ohm's law
modules.html:
Current I = Q/t (ampere). Ohm's law: V = IR (at constant T).

Resistance R = ρL/A (ρ = resistivity, Ω·m). Metals: R increases with T; semiconductors: R decreases with T.

Series: R = R₁+R₂+...; same current. Parallel: 1/R = 1/R₁+1/R₂+...; same voltage.

Electrical energy W = VIt = I²Rt = V²t/R. Power P = VI = I²R = V²/R.

examples.q: Two resistors 4 Ω and 6 Ω in series across 20 V. Current?

modules.title: EMF, internal resistance, potentiometer
modules.html:
EMF (ε) is the energy per unit charge supplied by a source. Terminal voltage V = ε − Ir where r is internal resistance.

A potentiometer measures unknown EMF by comparing balance lengths on a uniform resistance wire; no current flows through the unknown cell at balance.

examples.q: ε=6 V, r=0.5 Ω supplying 2 A. Terminal V?
Advantage of a potentiometer over a voltmeter?
examples.a: Draws no current at balance; gives true EMF.

modules.title: Electrolysis and cells
modules.html:
Electrolysis: passing current through an ionic solution decomposes it. Faraday's laws: (1) mass deposited m ∝ Q = It; (2) for same Q, masses ∝ chemical equivalents.

m = ZIt (Z = electrochemical equivalent).

Primary cells (non-rechargeable): dry Leclanché, alkaline. Secondary cells (rechargeable): lead-acid accumulator, Li-ion.

examples.q: Z for copper = 3.3×10⁻⁷ kg/C. Mass deposited by 2 A in 10 min?

modules.title: Magnetism
modules.html:
Magnets have N and S poles; like repel, unlike attract. Earth acts as a giant magnet; magnetic north lies near geographic south of Earth.

Dip (inclination): angle between magnetic field and horizontal. Declination: angle between magnetic and geographic meridian.

Field lines run N → S outside a magnet.

examples.a: Angle between Earth's field and horizontal.

examples.q: Poles of a bar magnet: like poles…

modules.title: Electromagnetism and induction; transformers
modules.html:
Current in a conductor produces a magnetic field. A current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field experiences a force F = BIL sinθ (Fleming's left-hand rule).

Electromagnetic induction: a changing magnetic flux induces an EMF. Faraday: EMF ∝ rate of change of flux. Lenz: induced current opposes the change.

Transformer: V_s/V_p = N_s/N_p. For an ideal transformer P_in = P_out → I_p V_p = I_s V_s.

examples.q: Step-down transformer: N_p=1000, N_s=200, V_p=240 V. V_s?
Which rule gives the direction of the induced EMF?
